records.ety: [OE. deshonour, dishonour, OF. deshonor, deshonur, F. dshonneur; pref. des- (L. dis-) + honor, honur, F. honneur, fr. L. honor. See Honor.]

defs: Lack of honor; disgrace; ignominy; shame; reproach.
quotes: It was not meet for us to see the king's dishonor.
His honor rooted in dishonor stood.

defs: The nonpayment or nonacceptance of commercial paper by the party on whom it is drawn.

records.ety: [OE. deshonouren, F. dshonorer; pref. ds- (L. dis-) + honorer to honor, fr. L. honorare. See Honor, v. t.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Dishonored (ds*n"rd or dz*n"rd); p. pr. & vb. n. Dishonoring.]

defs: To deprive of honor; to disgrace; to bring reproach or shame on; to treat with indignity, or as unworthy in the sight of others; to stain the character of; to lessen the reputation of; as, the duelist dishonors himself to maintain his honor.
quotes: Nothing . . . that may dishonor Our law, or stain my vow of Nazarite.

defs: To violate the chastity of; to debauch.
To refuse or decline to accept or pay; -- said of a bill, check, note, or draft which is due or presented; as, to dishonor a bill exchange.
